Tier 3 is the full reference guide (linked from Vale error messages) for edge cases and rationale. Content-type-specific templates (tutorial, reference, blog) encode structure rules as starter files

questions.explanation: The 3-tier architecture matches engineer behavior: most will read only the quick reference, CI automation catches violations without requiring memorization, and the full guide serves as a reference when needed. Content-type templates encode structural decisions, reducing cognitive overhead for non-writer contributors.

questions.question: A documentation platform serves content in 8 languages. The i18n workflow currently uses a translation memory (TM) system with human translators. You need to add 3 new languages with limited budget. Step 1: Evaluate the content corpus for machine-translatability. Step 2: Design a tiered quality strategy. Step 3: Define the feedback loop for continuous improvement.

choices.A: Step 1: Audit content by type -- API reference and UI strings have high repeatability (good for MT), conceptual guides have moderate complexity, marketing content has low translatability. Step 2: Tier the strategy -- critical paths (getting started, auth, billing) get MTPE (machine translation + human post-edit), high-volume reference gets MT with community review flags, and low-traffic pages get MT-only with prominent 'machine translated' badges. Step 3: Track translation quality scores per page, route user-reported issues to the TM for correction, and progressively promote MT-only pages to MTPE as the TM improves for each language pair
choices.B: Use machine translation for all 3 new languages with no human review
choices.C: Translate only the top 20 pages for each new language and leave the rest in English
choices.D: Wait until budget is available for full human translation of all 3 languages

questions.explanation: A tiered quality strategy allocates translation budget where it has the most impact: critical user paths get human-reviewed MT, high-volume reference gets community-reviewed MT, and low-traffic pages get flagged MT-only. The feedback loop progressively improves quality as the translation memory grows from corrections.

questions.question: You need to design a documentation quality dashboard for executive reporting. The dashboard must answer: 'Is our documentation helping users succeed, and where are the gaps?' Step 1: Define the key metrics. Step 2: Map data sources. Step 3: Design the dashboard layout for a non-technical audience.

choices.A: Show only page view counts and publication velocity
choices.B: Display only the number of documentation pages published per quarter
choices.C: Key metrics in 4 quadrants: Coverage (% of features documented, % of API endpoints documented), Quality (readability scores, style guide compliance rate, broken link count), Effectiveness (task completion rates, search success rate, support ticket deflection), and Freshness (pages reviewed within SLA, pages flagged as stale). Data sources: docs site analytics, CI lint results, support ticket system, search logs, Git commit history. Layout: traffic-light summary cards at top, trend lines for each metric, drill-down tables for stale/undocumented items
choices.D: Show raw analytics data from Google Analytics with no interpretation layer

questions.explanation: A documentation quality dashboard for executives needs to connect docs metrics to business outcomes. Four quadrants (coverage, quality, effectiveness, freshness) answer different executive questions. Traffic-light summaries enable at-a-glance assessment, trend lines show trajectory, and drill-downs enable action.

questions.question: You are documenting a real-time WebSocket API that has: connection lifecycle events (open, close, error, reconnect), message types (subscribe, unsubscribe, heartbeat, data), and back-pressure mechanisms (slow consumer detection, buffer overflow behavior). Traditional REST API documentation patterns (request/response per endpoint) do not fit. Design the documentation structure for this API.

choices.A: Document each message type as if it were a REST endpoint with request/response
choices.B: Write a single page listing all message types in a table with no lifecycle documentation
choices.C: Provide only a WebSocket URL and let developers use browser DevTools to discover the protocol
choices.D: Structure around the connection lifecycle: (1) Connection guide (handshake, authentication, reconnection strategy with exponential backoff). (2) Message reference organized by direction (client-to-server: subscribe, unsubscribe, heartbeat; server-to-client: data, error, system). (3) Protocol behavior (message ordering guarantees, back-pressure signals, buffer limits). (4) State machine diagram showing connection states and transitions. (5) Integration examples showing real subscription flows with error handling

questions.explanation: WebSocket APIs require documentation structured around connection lifecycle and bidirectional message flow, not REST-style endpoints. The connection guide covers the stateful nature, directional message reference clarifies the protocol, behavior documentation covers non-obvious semantics (ordering, back-pressure), and the state machine diagram shows the full lifecycle visually.

questions.question: Your documentation codebase has grown to 2,000 Markdown files across 8 repositories. Link checking takes 45 minutes in CI, broken internal links appear weekly, and cross-repo references break silently when pages are renamed. Step 1: Diagnose why the current approach fails at scale. Step 2: Design a link integrity system. Step 3: Optimize CI performance.

choices.A: Step 1: The current approach fails because link checking is monolithic (checks all links every run) and cross-repo references use raw URLs that bypass build-time validation. Step 2: Implement a link registry that maps semantic identifiers to URLs, updated at build time by each repo; cross-repo references use semantic IDs resolved at build time, so renames update the registry rather than breaking consumers. Step 3: Use incremental link checking (only check links on changed pages + their reverse dependencies), cache external link validation results with TTL, and parallelize checks across repos
choices.B: Disable link checking to fix the CI performance problem
choices.C: Replace all cross-repo links with 'see other repository' text instructions
choices.D: Move all 2,000 files into a single repository to eliminate cross-repo references

questions.explanation: The root cause is two-fold: raw URL references that break on rename, and monolithic link checking that does not scale. A semantic link registry makes references rename-proof, while incremental checking (only changed pages + dependents) plus caching reduces CI time from O(all links) to O(changed links).

questions.question: You are responsible for documenting a platform migration from REST API v2 to v3. 5,000 developers use v2 in production. The migration involves: breaking changes to 12 endpoints, new authentication (OAuth 2.0 replacing API keys), and a 6-month deprecation timeline. Design the documentation strategy for the migration. Consider: minimizing support burden, supporting incremental migration, and providing confidence to enterprise customers.

choices.A: Publish v3 documentation and remove v2 documentation immediately
choices.B: Send an email to all 5,000 developers with the complete list of breaking changes
choices.C: Create a migration hub with: (1) a migration overview page (timeline, breaking changes summary, risk assessment for enterprise customers), (2) per-endpoint migration guides showing v2 request/response alongside v3 equivalent with diff highlighting, (3) an authentication migration guide with step-by-step OAuth 2.0 setup, (4) a compatibility matrix showing which v2 features map to v3, (5) codemods or migration scripts with documentation, and (6) v2 deprecation warnings in the v2 docs linking to the corresponding v3 migration guide for each endpoint
choices.D: Document only v3 and let developers discover breaking changes through runtime errors

questions.explanation: A migration hub centralizes the migration experience: the overview sets expectations for decision-makers, per-endpoint guides enable incremental migration, the auth guide addresses the most impactful change, the compatibility matrix enables impact assessment, and in-context deprecation warnings in v2 docs provide just-in-time migration guidance.

questions.question: A knowledge base with 1,200 articles has grown organically over 5 years. Users report difficulty finding content, and search analytics show 30% of searches result in zero hits despite relevant content existing. Step 1: Diagnose the information architecture failures. Step 2: Design a taxonomy restructure. Step 3: Plan the migration without breaking existing bookmarks and search rankings.

choices.A: Delete all articles and start from scratch with a new taxonomy
choices.B: Reorganize articles alphabetically by title
choices.C: Add a better search engine without changing the content structure
choices.D: Step 1: Audit reveals duplicated content under inconsistent categories, synonym-divergent terminology (same concept called different names), and orphaned articles with no category assignment -- all degrading search recall. Step 2: Design a controlled vocabulary mapping existing terms to canonical terms, restructure into a 3-level hierarchy with cross-cutting tags, and create redirect rules for consolidated pages. Step 3: Implement URL redirects (301) for all moved pages, run search re-indexing, preserve canonical URLs where possible, update internal cross-references via automated rewriting, and monitor search zero-result rate as the success metric

questions.explanation: The 30% zero-hit rate indicates a terminology mismatch between user queries and content, plus structural issues hiding relevant content. A controlled vocabulary fixes the semantic gap, hierarchy restructuring improves browsability, and 301 redirects preserve existing bookmarks and SEO equity during migration.

questions.question: You are implementing a PlantUML-to-Mermaid migration for a documentation site to eliminate the PlantUML server dependency. The documentation contains 85 diagrams: 40 sequence diagrams, 20 class diagrams, 15 activity diagrams, and 10 component diagrams. Step 1: Identify which diagram types migrate cleanly. Step 2: Identify which require redesign. Step 3: Design the migration workflow.

choices.A: Step 1: Sequence and class diagrams have near-direct Mermaid equivalents (migrate with syntax transformation). Step 2: Activity diagrams map to Mermaid flowcharts with some layout compromises; component diagrams have no direct Mermaid equivalent and require redesign as C4 diagrams (using mermaid-c4 or flowchart subgraphs). Step 3: Write automated syntax converters for the 60 directly-mappable diagrams, manually redesign the 25 that need rethinking, visual-diff validate each migration, and batch the work across sprints prioritized by page traffic
choices.B: Manually redraw all 85 diagrams in Mermaid from scratch
choices.C: Keep PlantUML for diagrams that do not convert and run both rendering systems
choices.D: Replace all diagrams with text descriptions to avoid the migration

questions.explanation: Not all PlantUML diagram types have Mermaid equivalents. Sequence and class diagrams convert directly, activity diagrams need flowchart adaptation, and component diagrams require architectural redesign. Automating the direct conversions (70% of diagrams) and manually redesigning the rest is the efficient approach.

questions.question: You are designing an MDX-based documentation system that embeds live API explorers. The explorers allow users to input parameters and see real API responses. Step 1: What are the security implications? Step 2: How do you handle authentication in the explorer? Step 3: How do you ensure the interactive component does not degrade the documentation experience for non-interactive users?

choices.A: Step 1: Security risks include exposing API keys in client-side code, enabling unintended production mutations, and potential XSS through response rendering. Step 2: Use a sandbox API environment with read-only tokens, or proxy requests through a rate-limited documentation backend that strips sensitive headers. Step 3: Render a static default response below the interactive component as the SSR fallback, use progressive enhancement (static content loads first, interactive layer hydrates), and ensure all information conveyed by the explorer is also available in static text for accessibility and offline readers
choices.B: Embed live API calls directly against the production API with no authentication
choices.C: Require users to paste their production API key into the explorer
choices.D: Only show the API explorer to authenticated documentation users

questions.explanation: Live API explorers introduce security surface area (key exposure, production mutations, XSS), authentication complexity (sandbox vs proxy), and accessibility concerns (interactive-only content excludes some users). Sandboxed environments, progressive enhancement, and static fallbacks address all three dimensions.

questions.question: Your team writes documentation in English, which is then translated into 6 languages. A new feature requires documenting culturally-sensitive financial concepts (credit scores, tax identification numbers) that differ fundamentally across regions. Step 1: Why does standard translation fail here? Step 2: What is the documentation strategy? Step 3: How do you manage the content lifecycle across locales?

choices.A: Translate the English documentation directly and add footnotes for regional differences
choices.B: Step 1: Standard translation fails because the concepts themselves differ (credit scores do not exist in some regions; tax ID formats vary). Direct translation produces incorrect or meaningless content. Step 2: Use locale-specific content authoring (not translation) for region-dependent sections: define a shared structural template, but have regional subject matter experts write the locale-specific content natively. Step 3: Track content freshness per locale independently, since region-specific regulations change on different schedules; use a content matrix mapping shared components vs locale-specific components, with independent review cycles for each locale's regulatory content
choices.C: Remove all culturally-sensitive content from the documentation
choices.D: Use a single global description and tell users to consult their local regulations

questions.explanation: When concepts differ across cultures (not just words), translation is the wrong tool -- locale-specific authoring by regional SMEs is needed. A shared template ensures structural consistency while allowing each locale to describe their own financial concepts accurately. Independent freshness tracking handles asynchronous regulatory changes.

questions.question: You discover that a reference documentation page rendered from an OpenAPI spec has a table with 47 columns representing all fields of a complex response object. The table is unusable on mobile and overwhelming on desktop. Step 1: Why does the generated format fail? Step 2: Design an alternative presentation. Step 3: Ensure the alternative stays in sync with the OpenAPI spec.

choices.A: Remove the table and list only the 10 most important fields
choices.B: Step 1: Auto-generated flat tables cannot represent nested objects meaningfully, and 47 columns exceed human scanning capacity. Step 2: Replace with a hierarchical, collapsible schema explorer that groups fields by object nesting, shows required fields expanded by default and optional fields collapsed, with type badges and inline descriptions. Step 3: Generate the explorer component from the OpenAPI spec at build time using a custom renderer that reads the same `$ref`-resolved schema, ensuring any spec change is reflected automatically without manual updates
choices.C: Split the table across 5 separate pages with 9-10 columns each
choices.D: Convert the table to a PDF download that users can view separately

questions.explanation: Flat tables fail for complex nested schemas because they cannot represent hierarchy and overwhelm users with column count. A collapsible schema explorer respects the object structure, uses progressive disclosure (required fields visible, optional collapsed), and is generated from the same OpenAPI spec to maintain sync.

questions.question: You are building a docs-as-code pipeline for a monorepo containing 4 services, each with its own `docs/` directory. The pipeline must: (1) build a unified site from all 4 docs directories, (2) support service-specific navigation and versioning, (3) run PR previews showing only the changed service's docs, and (4) deploy to a CDN with cache invalidation scoped to changed paths only. Design the pipeline architecture.

choices.A: Build each service's docs independently with no unified site
choices.B: Use a monorepo-aware build system: a root `mkdocs.yml` uses the monorepo plugin to merge 4 service docs into a unified nav. PR preview builds use file-change detection to identify affected services and build only those (with stubs for unchanged services). Production builds generate the full site. CDN deployment uses content-hash-based cache keys so only changed pages invalidate. Service-specific `mike` versioning allows independent version releases per service within the unified site
choices.C: Copy all docs into a single flat directory and build from there
choices.D: Build the full site on every PR regardless of which service changed

questions.explanation: Monorepo documentation requires balancing unified output with service-level independence. The monorepo plugin merges navigation, change detection scopes PR previews (fast feedback), content-hash cache keys minimize CDN invalidation (performance), and per-service versioning allows independent release cycles within one site.

questions.question: A documentation site scores 92/100 on Lighthouse accessibility but receives complaints from screen reader users that navigation is confusing. The site uses: a persistent left sidebar with collapsible sections, breadcrumbs, a table of contents sidebar on the right, and a search modal. Step 1: Why might Lighthouse miss these issues? Step 2: What manual testing would reveal the problems? Step 3: What ARIA improvements would fix them?

choices.A: Lighthouse accessibility scoring is sufficient; the complaints are invalid
choices.B: Replace the sidebar navigation with a single hamburger menu
choices.C: Increase the Lighthouse score to 100 by fixing the remaining automated issues
choices.D: Step 1: Lighthouse tests automated rules (contrast, alt text, ARIA attributes) but cannot evaluate navigation coherence, focus management, or reading order -- issues that require human judgment. Step 2: Manual testing with a screen reader (NVDA/JAWS) reveals: sidebar focus traps on collapse/expand, no skip-navigation link to bypass the 50-item sidebar, breadcrumbs and TOC not marked as `nav` landmarks with descriptive `aria-label`, and search modal does not trap focus or announce itself. Step 3: Add skip-nav link, label `nav` landmarks distinctly ('Main navigation,' 'Breadcrumbs,' 'On this page'), implement focus management for sidebar collapse and search modal, and add `aria-current="page"` to the active sidebar item

questions.explanation: Lighthouse catches syntactic accessibility issues (missing attributes, contrast) but not semantic issues (navigation coherence, focus management, landmark labeling). Screen reader testing reveals the experiential problems: focus traps, missing skip links, unlabeled landmarks, and modal focus management -- all requiring manual ARIA improvements.

questions.question: Your organization wants to measure the ROI of documentation. Engineering leadership asks: 'How do we know our documentation investment is paying off?' Step 1: Define measurable outcomes tied to business value. Step 2: Design the measurement framework. Step 3: Address the attribution challenge (documentation is one of many factors affecting outcomes).

choices.A: Step 1: Tie docs to business metrics: support ticket deflection (fewer tickets = cost savings), time-to-first-API-call (faster onboarding = higher conversion), developer satisfaction scores, and self-service resolution rate. Step 2: Implement measurement: instrument docs with analytics to track user journeys, correlate doc page visits with support ticket reduction (users who read docs before filing tickets), measure time-to-first-successful-API-call for users who follow the quickstart vs those who do not, survey developer satisfaction quarterly. Step 3: Use controlled comparison (cohort of users with/without docs access), trend analysis (docs improvements correlated with metric changes over time), and acknowledge documentation as a contributing factor with confidence intervals rather than claiming sole attribution
choices.B: Report the number of documentation pages published as the ROI metric
choices.C: Claim that documentation prevents all support tickets
choices.D: Report only qualitative feedback ('developers like the docs') without quantitative metrics

questions.explanation: Documentation ROI requires connecting docs metrics to business outcomes (ticket deflection, onboarding speed, satisfaction). The attribution challenge -- docs are one of many factors -- is addressed through controlled comparisons and trend correlation rather than claiming sole causation. This gives leadership actionable, honest data.

questions.question: You are designing a documentation system for a regulated industry (medical devices, FDA 21 CFR Part 11). The documentation must: (1) maintain full audit trails for every content change, (2) require electronic signatures for publication approval, (3) support content recall (ability to retract published content), and (4) comply with records retention requirements. Evaluate how a docs-as-code system can meet these regulatory requirements vs a traditional document management system (DMS).

choices.A: Docs-as-code meets most requirements with augmentation: (1) Git provides immutable audit trails (every change attributed, timestamped, and linked to a commit). (2) Electronic signatures require a PR approval workflow with GPG-signed commits or an external e-signature integration that records approver identity and timestamp. (3) Content recall is implemented via tagged releases with the ability to roll back and redeploy, plus a 'withdrawn' status page replacing retracted content. (4) Records retention uses Git tag protection policies, branch protection, and periodic archival to a compliant long-term storage system. The gaps vs traditional DMS: Git does not natively enforce 21 CFR Part 11 e-signature requirements, requiring a wrapper tool or integration
choices.B: Docs-as-code cannot meet regulatory requirements; use a traditional DMS exclusively
choices.C: Use Google Docs for all regulated documentation since it has version history
choices.D: Regulatory compliance only applies to the product, not its documentation

questions.explanation: Git-based docs-as-code provides strong audit trails and version control that meet many regulatory requirements. The key gap is formal electronic signatures (21 CFR Part 11 requires specific identity verification that Git commits alone do not satisfy), which needs an integration layer. Understanding where docs-as-code is strong and where it needs augmentation enables a compliant hybrid approach.

questions.question: You inherit a developer portal with the following problems: (1) 40% of pages have not been updated in 18 months, (2) three different writing styles are evident from different eras of the company, (3) the navigation has 6 levels deep with 200+ leaf pages, (4) the search returns irrelevant results, and (5) developer NPS for documentation is -15. You have budget for one technical writer and 6 months. Step 1: Prioritize the problems. Step 2: Design a 6-month remediation plan. Step 3: Define success metrics for each phase.

choices.A: Rewrite all 200+ pages from scratch within 6 months
choices.B: Hire 5 contractors to address all problems simultaneously
choices.C: Focus exclusively on fixing search since that is the most technically interesting problem
choices.D: Step 1: Priority by developer impact: navigation depth (users cannot find content) > stale content (users find wrong answers) > search quality (degraded by stale/inconsistent content) > style inconsistency (cosmetic). Step 2: Months 1-2: Restructure navigation to 3 levels max, consolidate/archive stale pages (reduce to ~120 active pages), implement redirects. Months 3-4: Update the top-30 highest-traffic pages for accuracy and consistent style, implement a Vale pipeline for new content. Months 5-6: Improve search relevance (metadata, synonyms), establish a freshness review cycle, create contribution guide for engineers. Step 3: Month 2 success: navigation depth <= 3, page count reduced 40%. Month 4: top-30 pages pass Vale checks, stale content flagged < 20%. Month 6: search zero-result rate < 10%, developer NPS positive

questions.explanation: With one writer and 6 months, ruthless prioritization by developer impact is essential. Navigation restructuring and content pruning (months 1-2) have the highest ROI because they fix findability. Updating high-traffic pages (months 3-4) fixes accuracy where it matters most. Search and process improvements (months 5-6) sustain the gains. Each phase has measurable success criteria.
